heroOverview: A furuncle, commonly called a boil, is a deep bacterial infection of a hair follicle and the surrounding tissue, caused in the great majority of cases by Staphylococcus aureus. The infection begins in the follicle, then spreads into the dermis and subcutaneous fat to form a tender, pus-filled nodule that enlarges over several days. Furuncles occur on any hair-bearing skin, with the back of the neck, face, buttocks, thighs, and armpits the most common sites. They affect people of all ages but are most frequent in adolescents and young adults, and in people who carry S. aureus on their skin or nasal passages. Most furuncles come to a head and drain on their own within one to two weeks; larger or complicated boils often require incision and drainage. Methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA) accounts for up to 75% of community-acquired skin abscesses in some U.S. regions, making antibiotic choice clinically important.

keyStats.prevalence: Skin abscesses and furuncles account for approximately 3.3 million emergency department visits annually in the United States (CDC data)
keyStats.demographics: Most common in adolescents and adults aged 15-40; men affected slightly more than women; higher rates in people who carry nasal S. aureus
keyStats.globalCases: One of the most common bacterial skin infections worldwide; exact global incidence not systematically tracked

definition: A furuncle (ICD-10: L02.92) is an acute, deep-seated infection originating in a hair follicle that extends into the surrounding dermis and subcutaneous tissue, producing a tender, fluctuant nodule filled with pus and necrotic debris. It is distinct from a simple folliculitis, which remains confined to the upper portion of the follicle. Staphylococcus aureus, including community-associated methicillin-resistant S. aureus (CA-MRSA), is responsible for most furuncles. When multiple adjacent follicles fuse into a larger, interconnected network of tunnels and pus pockets, the lesion is called a carbuncle. Furuncles fall under dermatology for recurrent or problematic cases. Risk is substantially increased by nasal carriage of S. aureus, close contact with infected individuals, skin trauma, hyperhidrosis, obesity, diabetes, and immunosuppression. Most furuncles are self-limiting: the inflammatory process localises the infection, the central core liquefies, and the lesion points and drains spontaneously. However, large or deep lesions, those on the face or over the spine, and infections in immunocompromised patients can carry real risks including local spread, sepsis, and intracranial extension.

typesClassification.description: A single, self-contained pus-filled nodule arising from one infected follicle; most common presentation, often resolving with warm compresses and, if large, incision and drainage.
A coalescence of several adjacent furuncles forming a deeper, larger abscess with multiple follicular openings; more systemic toxicity, more likely to need antibiotics and formal drainage.
Three or more separate boil episodes within 12 months; often linked to S. aureus nasal carriage, household transmission, or underlying immune deficiency.

Infection caused by methicillin-resistant S. aureus; clinically indistinguishable from methicillin-susceptible furuncles but requires different antibiotic choice; now accounts for the majority of community skin abscesses in many regions.

primarySymptoms: A firm, red, painful nodule that typically starts as a pea-sized lump around a hair follicle and enlarges over three to five days to a cherry-sized or larger lesion.
Throbbing or pulsating pain at the site, which intensifies as pus accumulates and often eases abruptly when the boil ruptures and drains.
Central softening and fluctuance — a palpable sensation of fluid under tension — indicating that a pus-filled cavity has formed and the lesion is ready to drain.
A white or yellow 'head' or point visible at the centre of the lesion, representing the pus that is about to discharge through the skin surface.
Surrounding erythema and warmth that extends a centimetre or two beyond the central nodule, reflecting local inflammatory response in the dermis.
Localised swelling that may distort nearby anatomy, particularly on the face, neck, or axilla, where loose connective tissue allows oedema to spread.
Mild fever (typically 37.5–38.5°C) in about a third of patients, reflecting a systemic immune response even when the infection remains localised.
Regional lymph node enlargement — tender, swollen nodes in the groin for a thigh boil, or axillary nodes for an arm lesion — signalling early lymphatic involvement.
Crusting and bloody or purulent discharge at the skin surface once the boil has spontaneously ruptured or been drained.
Itching or tingling around the lesion during the early stage, sometimes before a distinct nodule can be seen or felt.
earlyWarningSigns: A tender, red papule or small bump developing around a hair follicle, particularly in a region where boils have occurred before.
Increasing redness that extends beyond a small follicular papule over 24-48 hours.
Localised warmth and subtle swelling before frank nodule formation.
A persistent 'deep pimple' that does not discharge after several days.
emergencySigns: Rapidly expanding redness beyond the boil margin with red streaking up a limb, suggesting cellulitis or lymphangitis spreading from the source.
High fever above 38.5°C, rigors, or rapid heart rate, which may indicate bacteraemia or early sepsis from a furuncle on the face or neck.
A boil located on the central face — the nose, upper lip, or cheek — with surrounding swelling or visual changes, because venous drainage in that zone can carry infection to the cavernous sinus.
Signs of sepsis: confusion, a pulse above 100, blood pressure drop, or feeling dramatically unwell alongside a skin boil.
A boil in a person who is immunocompromised (transplant recipient, on chemotherapy, taking high-dose steroids, or HIV-positive) that is enlarging despite 48 hours of appropriate antibiotics.

causes.cause: Staphylococcus aureus skin or nasal colonisation
causes.description: S. aureus, which asymptomatically colonises the nasal passages of roughly 30% of people and the skin in a further fraction, seeds individual follicles through minor abrasions, shaving, or friction; once inside the follicle it triggers an intense neutrophil response that liquefies surrounding tissue and forms the pus cavity characteristic of a furuncle.

CA-MRSA strains have acquired specific virulence factors, including Panton-Valentine leukocidin (PVL) toxin, which destroys white blood cells and promotes tissue necrosis; these strains spread person-to-person without healthcare contact and are now responsible for the majority of community skin abscesses in many parts of the United States and globally.

Shaving, waxing, friction from clothing, insect bites, and minor cuts breach the follicular barrier, providing a portal of entry for surface bacteria; the follicular canal becomes the initial site of multiplication before infection spreads deeper.

Excess sweating softens and macerates the follicular opening; occlusive clothing, dressings, or tight-fitting equipment keeps moisture against skin and creates a warm, humid microenvironment that facilitates bacterial overgrowth and follicular blockage.

Poorly controlled diabetes mellitus impairs neutrophil chemotaxis and intracellular killing of S. aureus; similarly, HIV infection, corticosteroid use, chemotherapy, and inherited neutrophil disorders all reduce the ability to contain follicular infection before it deepens into a furuncle.

Sharing towels, razors, sports equipment, or sleeping quarters with an infected individual transfers S. aureus or MRSA to skin surfaces; household clusters and sports-team outbreaks are well documented and are the primary mechanism of recurrent furunculosis within families.

riskFactors.description: Approximately 30% of the general population persistently carry S. aureus in their anterior nares; carriers have a two- to threefold higher rate of skin infections than non-carriers, and decolonisation with nasal mupirocin reduces recurrence in furunculosis protocols.

Poorly controlled diabetes impairs neutrophil function, promotes colonisation with S. aureus, and slows wound healing; infection risk correlates with glycaemic control, and improving HbA1c reduces skin infection frequency.

Excess weight creates skin folds that trap moisture and friction, provides occluded follicular environments, and is associated with mild immune dysregulation; adipose tissue also has reduced blood supply, limiting antibiotic penetration.
Living with or caring for someone who has recurrent or MRSA furuncles is a major risk factor for acquisition; MRSA can survive on surfaces and bedding and spreads through skin-to-skin contact.

HIV infection, organ transplantation, chemotherapy, and congenital neutrophil disorders all impair bacterial killing, allowing minor follicular colonisation to progress to frank abscess; furuncles in immunocompromised patients are more severe and more likely to bacteraemise.
Eczema, psoriasis, and other inflammatory dermatoses disrupt the skin barrier and alter the microbiome, creating entry points for S. aureus and reducing epidermal antimicrobial peptide expression.

Abrasions, skin-to-skin contact, and shared equipment in wrestling, football, and martial arts are well-documented vectors for CA-MRSA; outbreaks among sports teams have been reported in numerous countries.
Prior broad-spectrum antibiotic courses select for resistant organisms including MRSA and alter the skin microbiome, facilitating colonisation by more virulent strains.

affectedDemographics: Adolescents and young adults aged 15-40, who have higher rates of contact sports and hormonal changes affecting sebaceous glands
People who carry S. aureus in the nose or on the skin persistently
Individuals with poorly controlled diabetes mellitus
People who are obese, with deep skin folds in the axillae, groins, or inframammary regions
Household contacts of people with recurrent or MRSA furunculosis
Athletes in contact sports, particularly wrestlers and American football players
Immunocompromised patients, including those with HIV or on chemotherapy
People in crowded living conditions such as prisons, military barracks, or shelters
diagnosisOverview: Furuncles are diagnosed clinically based on the appearance of a tender, fluctuant, erythematous nodule arising from a hair follicle. No laboratory test is needed to confirm a straightforward boil in an otherwise healthy person. The key diagnostic decision is whether the lesion is fluctuant — that is, whether a pus cavity has formed that would benefit from drainage — and whether surrounding soft tissue infection (cellulitis) or systemic signs suggest a need for antibiotics or further investigation. Culture of the pus obtained at incision and drainage is recommended in most guidelines because it guides antibiotic selection, particularly for MRSA, and informs decolonisation strategies in recurrent cases. A swab from an intact boil surface is unreliable because it reflects colonising flora. Blood cultures are indicated if the patient has fever, rigors, or systemic signs of infection. In people with recurrent furunculosis, evaluation includes screening for nasal carriage (anterior nares swab), diabetes (fasting glucose or HbA1c), and, in selected patients, immune function testing. Imaging with ultrasound is useful when there is uncertainty about the extent of a deep or complex abscess, especially in anatomically constrained sites such as the perineum or axilla, to distinguish a simple furuncle from a deeper abscess, fascial involvement, or a foreign body.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirm the presence of a fluctuant, follicular-centred nodule and assess for surrounding cellulitis, lymphangitis, or systemic signs.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The clinician inspects and palpates the lesion; fluctuance — a wave-like sensation of fluid — indicates the lesion is ready for drainage. No special equipment is required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identify the causative organism (especially MRSA) and determine antibiotic susceptibility to guide targeted treatment and decolonisation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: During incision and drainage, pus is collected with a swab or syringe and sent to the microbiology laboratory; results typically return in 48-72 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Nasal swab for S. aureus carriage
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detect persistent nasal colonisation with S. aureus or MRSA, which drives recurrent furunculosis and can be addressed with decolonisation therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A cotton swab is gently rotated in each nostril; results return in 24-48 hours and distinguish methicillin-susceptible from resistant strains.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detect bacteraemia when systemic signs such as fever, rigors, tachycardia, or hypotension accompany the skin infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood sample is drawn from a vein, usually twice from different sites; results take 24-72 hours, though positive results are often flagged earlier.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screen for undiagnosed or poorly controlled diabetes mellitus, a common predisposing factor in recurrent furunculosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results are available within hours for glucose and within a day for HbA1c.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Differentiate a drainable abscess cavity from cellulitis without an abscess, and delineate the depth and extent of the collection before drainage, especially in the perineum, axilla, or neck.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel is applied to the skin and a small probe is moved across the area; the procedure is painless and takes five to ten minutes.

treatmentOverview: Most furuncles in healthy individuals resolve without intervention or with warm compresses applied for 15-20 minutes four times daily, which increase local blood flow, promote maturation of the lesion, and encourage spontaneous drainage. Once a boil is clearly fluctuant — soft and pus-filled — incision and drainage (I&D) is the definitive treatment and the most important intervention: studies consistently show that drainage alone cures the majority of uncomplicated abscesses without the need for antibiotics. The NEJM NEJM Nejm 2017 IDSA trial confirmed that adding trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX) to I&D does modestly improve cure rates and reduce recurrence, so most current guidelines recommend a 5-7 day course of an antibiotic active against CA-MRSA after drainage for moderate-to-large furuncles. Antibiotic treatment without drainage is generally insufficient for a fluctuant furuncle because antibiotics cannot penetrate a well-formed abscess cavity adequately. Oral antibiotics covering MRSA — TMP-SMX, doxycycline, or clindamycin depending on local susceptibility — are used for outpatient management. Intravenous antibiotics are reserved for the minority of patients with spreading cellulitis, systemic infection, failure of oral therapy, or compromised immunity. Recurrent furunculosis is treated with a structured decolonisation protocol that typically combines nasal mupirocin for five days, daily chlorhexidine body wash for five to seven days, and in household clusters, simultaneous decolonisation of all contacts. Excellent hand hygiene and avoidance of sharing personal items remain the cornerstone of prevention.

medicalTreatments.name: Warm compresses
medicalTreatments.description: Applied for 15-20 minutes four times daily to promote maturation and pointing of the lesion, encouraging spontaneous drainage of small furuncles before they reach the size requiring formal incision.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for early-stage lesions less than 2 cm; approximately 50% of small furuncles managed with compresses alone resolve without procedural intervention.

medicalTreatments.name: Incision and drainage (I&D)
medicalTreatments.description: The definitive treatment for a fluctuant furuncle: a scalpel incision opens the abscess cavity, pus and necrotic core are expressed, and the cavity is irrigated; most IDSA guidelines support I&D as primary treatment for uncomplicated furuncles.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate of approximately 80-85% for uncomplicated furuncles with drainage alone; adding antibiotics raises cure to about 92% in MRSA-predominant populations.

medicalTreatments.name: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX)
medicalTreatments.description: An oral antibiotic active against most CA-MRSA strains; current IDSA and guideline data support 5-7 days adjunctive to I&D for moderate-to-large furuncles and for patients at risk of spreading infection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the NEJM 2017 landmark trial, TMP-SMX plus I&D achieved a 92.9% cure rate versus 85.7% for I&D plus placebo — a 7-point absolute improvement that is clinically significant in high-risk patients.

medicalTreatments.name: Doxycycline
medicalTreatments.description: An oral tetracycline antibiotic with good CA-MRSA activity; used as an alternative to TMP-SMX, especially when sulfonamide allergy or intolerance is present; given for 5-7 days after drainage.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure rates of approximately 82-88% in observational studies of CA-MRSA skin infections, broadly comparable to TMP-SMX.

medicalTreatments.name: Clindamycin
medicalTreatments.description: An oral antibiotic with activity against most MRSA strains in regions where inducible clindamycin resistance is below 15-20%; used for 5-7 days, but local susceptibility data should be checked before prescribing empirically.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 80-85% cure in susceptible strains; effectiveness drops significantly in areas with high inducible resistance, so culture sensitivity testing is important.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous vancomycin
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for hospitalised patients with systemic infection, bacteraemia, deep tissue spread, or failure of oral MRSA-active antibiotics; the standard of care for serious MRSA infection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Highly effective against MRSA bacteraemia; bacteriological cure rates above 90% with appropriate dosing; requires monitoring of drug levels and renal function.

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage under local anaesthesia
surgicalOptions.description: For fluctuant furuncles and carbuncles in outpatient settings: local anaesthetic is injected around the lesion, a small incision is made over the point of maximal fluctuance, pus evacuated, loculations broken down, and the cavity irrigated with saline; the wound is usually left open or loosely packed to allow continued drainage.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolution in approximately 80-92% of cases depending on whether adjunct antibiotics are used; carbuncles may require a larger incision or repeat drainage.

surgicalOptions.name: Formal surgical drainage in theatre
surgicalOptions.description: For very large, deep, or complex abscesses — particularly perianal or perineal furuncles, axillary carbuncles, or infections in immunocompromised patients — where adequate drainage under local anaesthesia is not feasible or safe, surgical drainage under general or regional anaesthesia allows thorough exploration and debriding.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Definitive resolution in the majority of appropriately selected cases; recurrence relates to underlying predisposing conditions rather than technique.

alternativeTreatments.name: Decolonisation protocol
alternativeTreatments.description: For recurrent furunculosis: intranasal mupirocin 2% ointment twice daily for five days combined with daily chlorhexidine 4% body wash for 5-7 days, repeated monthly for three months; simultaneously decolonise household contacts and decontaminate shared surfaces.

alternativeTreatments.name: Tea tree oil
alternativeTreatments.description: Some small trials suggest topical tea tree oil (5% concentration) may reduce S. aureus carriage on skin; evidence is insufficient to recommend it as a primary treatment but it is unlikely to cause harm as a complementary measure on intact skin.

whySeeSpecialist: Most single furuncles are managed in primary care or urgent care, but a dermatologist should be consulted when boils recur three or more times a year, when carbuncles are large or inadequately drained, when decolonisation has been attempted and failed, or when an unusual causative organism is suspected. Immunocompromised patients with furuncles warrant specialist input early, as do patients with facial boils in the danger triangle or any boil complicated by spreading cellulitis, lymphangitis, or systemic infection.
doctorSelectionGuide: A dermatologist with experience in skin infections and bacterial dermatology is the appropriate specialist for recurrent furunculosis. For the initial episode, a primary care physician or urgent care clinician can perform incision and drainage and initiate antibiotics. If MRSA is confirmed, coordination with an infectious disease specialist is valuable to plan decolonisation and manage household contacts. Look for a practice that performs in-office drainage, sends routine wound cultures, and has a structured decolonisation protocol.
preventionStrategies: Practise thorough handwashing with soap and water or hand sanitiser, especially after touching any skin lesion.
Avoid sharing towels, razors, sports equipment, or clothing that touches skin, which are common vectors for S. aureus and MRSA.
Cover any open boil or drainage wound with a clean bandage to prevent spread to other skin areas or household contacts.
Complete a formal decolonisation course with mupirocin and chlorhexidine if you have recurrent furunculosis or confirmed MRSA carriage.
Control underlying diabetes with diet, exercise, and medication to normalise immune function.
Wash workout clothes and shared sports gear after every use; shower promptly after contact sports.
lifestyleModifications: Keep skin clean and dry, particularly in skin folds and hair-bearing areas prone to sweating.
Use a clean towel daily and avoid sharing it with other household members.
Change and launder bedding at least weekly, using hot water when active furunculosis is present.
Shave with a clean, single-use razor and in the direction of hair growth to minimise follicular microtrauma.
Manage weight, as obesity is an independent risk factor for recurring skin infections in fold-bearing areas.
Inform close contacts and sexual partners if MRSA is confirmed so they can be screened and decolonised simultaneously.

recommended: A balanced diet supporting immune function, including adequate protein, zinc, and vitamins A, C, and D
Sufficient hydration to maintain skin integrity and support circulation
Foods that support glycaemic control if diabetes is a co-existing condition — whole grains, legumes, vegetables
avoid: High glycaemic foods and refined sugars if you have diabetes or impaired glucose tolerance, as poor glycaemic control directly impairs neutrophil function
Alcohol in excess, which impairs immune response and wound healing

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is not restricted during a furuncle, but contact sports, swimming pools, and shared gym equipment should be avoided until the wound is fully healed and dry to prevent spreading S. aureus or MRSA to other people or other skin areas. Cover any active lesion with a waterproof bandage before non-contact exercise. Return to contact sports only after the wound has closed completely.
dailyManagement: Apply warm, moist compresses for 15-20 minutes four times daily if the boil has not yet been drained.
Never squeeze or lance a boil at home — this risks pushing bacteria deeper, causing bacteraemia, or spreading infection to adjacent follicles.
If drainage has occurred, keep the wound covered with a clean bandage and change it twice daily.
Take the full course of prescribed antibiotics even if the wound appears to be healing.
Monitor the wound and surrounding skin daily for signs of spreading redness, increasing pain, or fever.
Wash hands thoroughly after touching or dressing any skin lesion.
prognosis: The outlook for an isolated furuncle in an otherwise healthy person is excellent. Most boils resolve completely within one to two weeks with or without drainage, leaving minimal scarring. After incision and drainage, pain typically improves within 24-48 hours and the wound closes over five to seven days. Recurrence is the main challenge: roughly 20-30% of people with one furuncle develop another within a year, and those with confirmed nasal carriage or household MRSA exposure are at ongoing risk. Structured decolonisation reduces recurrence by approximately 50% in controlled studies. Complications including cellulitis, bacteraemia, and sepsis are uncommon in healthy adults but are important to recognise early. Boils in the central facial danger triangle carry a small but serious risk of cavernous sinus thrombosis if squeezed or inadequately treated.
recoveryTimeline: Pain and swelling begin to ease within 24-48 hours after incision and drainage. Wound closure typically takes five to seven days for a simple furuncle drained in an outpatient setting; larger carbuncles may require 10-14 days of wound care. A mild residual firmness or scar may persist for several weeks as the dermis heals. Patients on oral antibiotics usually notice improvement in surrounding redness within two to three days of starting treatment.
complications: Cellulitis — spread of infection from the furuncle into the surrounding dermis and subcutaneous tissue; manifests as expanding redness, warmth, and induration beyond the boil margin.
Carbuncle formation — coalescence of multiple adjacent furuncles into a larger, multi-headed abscess that is more painful, more difficult to drain, and more likely to require antibiotics.

Bacteraemia and sepsis — entry of S. aureus or MRSA into the bloodstream, particularly from facial boils, manipulation of an unripe lesion, or infection in immunocompromised patients; can seed distant sites including the heart valves, joints, and spinal discs.

Cavernous sinus thrombosis — a rare but life-threatening complication specific to boils in the central facial triangle (nose, upper lip, inner cheeks); infected venous drainage tracks through valveless veins to the cavernous sinus.
Recurrent furunculosis — a pattern of three or more boil episodes per year driven by S. aureus nasal carriage, household transmission, or immune deficiency; significantly impacts quality of life.
Scarring and post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation — particularly in darker skin tones, where furuncles on visible areas of the face, neck, or chest can leave lasting cosmetic changes.

supportResources.name: CDC — MRSA Information for Patients
supportResources.description: Plain-English guide from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention on community MRSA, including skin infections, prevention, and decolonisation.

supportResources.name: NHS — Boils, Carbuncles and Skin Abscesses
supportResources.description: NHS patient information on causes, treatment, and when to seek medical help for boils and abscesses.

supportResources.name: IDSA — Practice Guidelines for Skin and Soft Tissue Infections
supportResources.description: Evidence-based clinical practice guidelines for clinicians managing skin infections, including furunculosis and MRSA.

sources.title: Liu C et al. IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Management of MRSA Infections (2011)
Talan DA et al. Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole versus Placebo for Uncomplicated Skin Abscess. NEJM 2017
Stevens DL et al. IDSA Practice Guidelines for Skin and Soft Tissue Infections (2014 update)
Daum RS. Skin and Soft Tissue Infections Caused by MRSA. NEJM 2007
CDC — MRSA in Healthcare and Community Settings
NHS — Boils, Carbuncles and Skin Abscesses: Treatment
Gorwitz RJ et al. Strategies for Clinical Management of MRSA in the Community. CDC 2006

confusedWithConditions.name: Acne vulgaris
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acne papules and pustules arise from sebaceous follicles mainly on the face and trunk; they are usually multiple, smaller, and lack the deep fluctuant pus cavity and surrounding cellulitis of a furuncle.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cellulitis of the face
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cellulitis is a diffuse, non-fluctuant spreading infection without a pus-filled central cavity; a furuncle is a discrete, fluctuant nodule, though cellulitis can develop as a complication around it.

confusedWithConditions.name: Impetigo
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Impetigo is a superficial skin infection producing honey-crusted erosions or thin-roofed blisters on the skin surface; it does not form a deep pus-filled nodule from a hair follicle.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eczema
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eczema causes chronic or relapsing itchy, dry, inflamed skin without focal pus-filled nodules; secondary bacterial infection of eczema can produce crusting but is distinct from a furuncle.

relatedConditions.name: Cellulitis of the face
relatedConditions.relevance: Cellulitis is both the main differential diagnosis and the most common local complication of an untreated or inadequately drained furuncle.

relatedConditions.name: Carbuncle
relatedConditions.relevance: A carbuncle is the next stage of severity from a furuncle: multiple adjacent boils coalesce into a larger, deeper abscess.

relatedConditions.name: Impetigo
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are superficial-to-deep bacterial skin infections caused predominantly by S. aureus; impetigo affects children most and stays superficial.

relatedConditions.name: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: A severe systemic complication of S. aureus infection in which exfoliative toxins cause widespread skin separation; shares the same pathogen as furunculosis.

faqs.question: What is a furuncle?
faqs.answer: A furuncle, or boil, is a deep bacterial infection of a hair follicle that forms a painful, pus-filled lump under the skin. It is most often caused by Staphylococcus aureus, including antibiotic-resistant MRSA strains.

faqs.question: How do I know if a boil is ready to drain?
faqs.answer: A boil is ready to drain when it becomes soft and fluctuant — you can feel a wave of fluid when gentle pressure is applied — and develops a visible white or yellow centre. This usually occurs 3-7 days after the lump appears.

faqs.question: Should I squeeze or pop a boil at home?
faqs.answer: No. Squeezing a boil can push bacteria deeper into the tissue, spread infection to adjacent skin, or trigger bacteraemia. Apply warm compresses to encourage natural drainage. If the boil is large or painful, see a clinician for proper incision and drainage.

faqs.question: Are boils contagious?
faqs.answer: The bacteria that cause boils — Staphylococcus aureus and MRSA — spread by skin-to-skin contact and through shared items like towels and razors. Cover any open boil with a bandage, wash hands frequently, and avoid sharing personal items to prevent spread.

faqs.question: What is the best antibiotic for a furuncle?
faqs.answer: For MRSA-associated furuncles, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole or doxycycline for 5-7 days is the first-line oral choice after drainage. For methicillin-susceptible infections, cefalexin or dicloxacillin is effective. Antibiotic choice should be guided by wound culture results and local resistance patterns.

faqs.question: Do all boils need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: No. Small furuncles under about 2 cm in a healthy person often resolve with warm compresses and incision and drainage alone. Antibiotics are added for moderate-to-large abscesses, spreading cellulitis, fever, immunocompromised patients, or when MRSA is confirmed or suspected.

faqs.question: Why do I keep getting boils?
faqs.answer: Recurrent furunculosis is most often driven by persistent nasal carriage of S. aureus or MRSA, household transmission from a contact who is also colonised, or an underlying condition like diabetes or eczema that impairs skin defences. A decolonisation course and household screening can break the cycle.

faqs.question: How is a boil treated by a doctor?
faqs.answer: The main treatment is incision and drainage: the clinician numbs the area with local anaesthetic, makes a small cut over the boil, evacuates the pus, irrigates the cavity, and may loosely pack the wound. A wound culture is taken to identify the bacteria. Antibiotics may be added for larger or complicated boils.

faqs.question: Can a boil on the face be dangerous?
faqs.answer: Boils on the central face — the nose, upper lip, and inner cheeks — are in a venous danger zone: infected blood can drain toward the brain. Never squeeze a facial boil. Seek prompt medical attention if a facial boil is enlarging, or if you develop eye swelling, headache, or fever.

faqs.question: How long does a furuncle take to heal?
faqs.answer: After incision and drainage, pain usually improves within 24-48 hours. Wound closure takes 5-7 days for a simple boil. Carbuncles and larger abscesses may take 10-14 days. A small firm scar may persist for several weeks as the skin fully heals.

faqs.question: What is decolonisation for recurrent boils?
faqs.answer: Decolonisation is a protocol to eliminate S. aureus or MRSA from the skin and nasal passages. It typically uses mupirocin antibiotic ointment in the nostrils twice daily for 5 days and chlorhexidine body wash daily for 5-7 days. All household contacts are treated at the same time for best results.

faqs.question: Is MRSA always the cause of boils?
faqs.answer: Not always, but MRSA causes the majority of community skin abscesses in many parts of the United States — estimates range from 50-75% in some regions. Treatment should cover MRSA until culture results are available, particularly if standard antibiotics have failed or if a household contact has confirmed MRSA.

faqs.question: What does a boil look like at different stages?
faqs.answer: In the first 1-2 days a boil looks like a firm red pimple. Over days 3-5 it enlarges into a tender, warm nodule. By days 5-7 it develops a soft centre and a visible yellow-white head. After it drains, a crater-like wound remains that closes over the following week.

faqs.question: Can diabetes cause boils?
faqs.answer: Yes. Poorly controlled diabetes impairs the ability of white blood cells to fight S. aureus and slows wound healing, increasing both the frequency and severity of furuncles. Improving blood sugar control with diet, exercise, and medication reduces skin infection risk.

faqs.question: When should I go to hospital for a boil?
faqs.answer: Go to hospital if you have a high fever or rigors, rapidly spreading redness with red streaking, a boil on the central face with swelling, signs of sepsis such as confusion or low blood pressure, or if you are immunocompromised and the boil is enlarging despite 48 hours of antibiotics.

faqs.question: Can children get furuncles?
faqs.answer: Yes. Children, particularly school-age children, can develop furuncles, often through close contact with colonised classmates or family members. CA-MRSA skin infections in children are well documented. Treatment principles are the same as in adults, with age-appropriate antibiotic dosing.

faqs.question: Will a furuncle leave a scar?
faqs.answer: Smaller boils that drain naturally or are incised cleanly often heal with minimal scarring. Larger carbuncles or repeatedly squeezed lesions are more likely to leave a pitted scar or area of post-inflammatory darkening, particularly in people with naturally darker skin tones.

faqs.question: What is the difference between a furuncle and a carbuncle?
faqs.answer: A furuncle is a single infected hair follicle forming one pus pocket. A carbuncle forms when several adjacent furuncles merge into a larger abscess with multiple drainage points; carbuncles are deeper, more painful, more likely to cause fever, and usually require formal surgical drainage and antibiotics.

faqs.question: How do I prevent boils from coming back?
faqs.answer: Prevent recurrence by keeping skin clean, not sharing towels or razors, covering wounds, completing a decolonisation course if MRSA-positive, controlling diabetes or other underlying conditions, and washing sports gear after every use. Treat all household contacts at the same time to stop reinfection.

faqs.question: Can a boil spread to other parts of my body?
faqs.answer: Yes. Bacteria from a boil can spread to nearby follicles causing new boils, to the surrounding skin causing cellulitis, or in rare cases into the bloodstream causing bacteraemia that may seed distant sites such as heart valves or bones. This is why self-squeezing is strongly discouraged.

metaDescription: A furuncle (boil) is a deep S. aureus skin infection causing a painful pus-filled nodule. Learn causes, drainage treatment and MRSA prevention. Doctor-reviewed.
